inputs.story: A series of bizarre UFO sightings have taken place in Byron, California this past week. Using only the clues below, match each sighting to its witness, date and location, and determine the reported shape of the unidentified object. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. Benny Baron's sighting was submitted 2 days after Dan Delgado's report.
2. Of the August 7 report and the August 5 sighting, one was reported by Gil Gates and the other was from Walnut Creek.
3. The report from Crescent City was submitted 1 day after the sighting from Islesboro.
4. The sighting from Walnut Creek was either the August 7 report or Edith Estes's sighting.
inputs.constants:
witnesses: Benny Baron; Dan Delgado; Edith Estes; Gil Gates.
dates: August 4; August 5; August 6; August 7.
towns: Crescent City; Embden; Islesboro; Walnut Creek.
inputs.solution:
Dates	Witnesses	Towns
August 4	Dan Delgado	Embden
August 5	Edith Estes	Walnut Creek
August 6	Benny Baron	Islesboro
August 7	Gil Gates	Crescent City

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

<problem_to_solve>
A series of bizarre UFO sightings have taken place in Byron, California this past week. Using only the clues below, match each sighting to its witness, date and location, and determine the reported shape of the unidentified object. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Benny Baron's sighting was submitted 2 days after Dan Delgado's report.
2. Of the August 7 report and the August 5 sighting, one was reported by Gil Gates and the other was from Walnut Creek.
3. The report from Crescent City was submitted 1 day after the sighting from Islesboro.
4. The sighting from Walnut Creek was either the August 7 report or Edith Estes's sighting.

Constants:
witnesses: Benny Baron; Dan Delgado; Edith Estes; Gil Gates.
dates: August 4; August 5; August 6; August 7.
towns: Crescent City; Embden; Islesboro; Walnut Creek.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

asp_generation.response:
To solve this logic grid puzzle, we need to match each UFO sighting to its witness, date, and location (town) based on the given clues. The solution involves encoding the problem into an Answer Set Programming (ASP) program that can be solved using Clingo. The key is to represent the categories (witnesses, dates, towns) and enforce the constraints from the clues.

### Approach
1. **Constants Definition**: Define the categories (witnesses, dates, towns) with their respective constants. Dates are converted to integers (e.g., August 4 → 4) for easier arithmetic operations.
2. **Search Space**: For each witness, assign exactly one date and one town using a choice rule.
3. **Uniqueness Constraint**: Ensure no two sightings share the same witness, date, and town (which is inherently handled by the choice rule and tuple uniqueness).
4. **Clue Encoding**:
   - **Clue 1**: Benny Baron's sighting is 2 days after Dan Delgado's. This is enforced by ensuring the date of Benny Baron's sighting equals Dan Delgado's date plus 2.
   - **Clue 2**: The August 7 report and August 5 sighting must involve Gil Gates and Walnut Creek in one of two specific ways. This is enforced by a constraint that forbids the assignment where neither condition holds.
   - **Clue 3**: The Crescent City report is 1 day after the Islesboro sighting. This is enforced by ensuring the date of Crescent City equals Islesboro's date plus 1.
   - **Clue 4**: The Walnut Creek sighting is either on August 7 or reported by Edith Estes. This is enforced by a constraint that forbids the assignment where Walnut Creek is neither on August 7 nor reported by Edith Estes.

### Solution Code
```asp
% Constants
witness("Benny Baron"; "Dan Delgado"; "Edith Estes"; "Gil Gates").
date(4; 5; 6; 7).
town("Crescent City"; "Embden"; "Islesboro"; "Walnut Creek").

% Search space: for each witness, assign exactly one date and one town.
{ sighting(W, D, T) : date(D), town(T) } = 1 :- witness(W).

% Uniqueness: no two sightings share the same witness, date, and town.
{ W1=W2; D1=D2; T1=T2 } = 0 :- sighting(W1,D1,T1), sighting(W2,D2,T2), (W1,D1,T1) != (W2,D2,T2).

% 1. Benny Baron's sighting was submitted 2 days after Dan Delgado's report.
:- sighting("Benny Baron", D1, _), sighting("Dan Delgado", D2, _), D1 != D2 + 2.

% 2. Of the August 7 report and the August 5 sighting, one was reported by Gil Gates and the other was from Walnut Creek.
:- sighting(W7, 7, T7), sighting(W5, 5, T5), 
   W7 != "Gil Gates", 
   T5 != "Walnut Creek",
   T7 != "Walnut Creek",
   W5 != "Gil Gates".

% 3. The report from Crescent City was submitted 1 day after the sighting from Islesboro.
:- sighting(_, D_cc, "Crescent City"), sighting(_, D_is, "Islesboro"), D_cc != D_is + 1.

% 4. The sighting from Walnut Creek was either the August 7 report or Edith Estes's sighting.
:- sighting(W, D, "Walnut Creek"), D != 7, W != "Edith Estes".

#show sighting/3.
```

### Explanation
1. **Constants**: The categories `witness`, `date`, and `town` are defined with their respective values. Dates are represented as integers (4 for August 4, etc.).
2. **Search Space**: The choice rule `{sighting(W, D, T) : date(D), town(T)} = 1 :- witness(W).` ensures each witness is assigned exactly one date and one town.
3. **Uniqueness**: The rule `{W1=W2; D1=D2; T1=T2} = 0 :- sighting(W1,D1,T1), sighting(W2,D2,T2), (W1,D1,T1) != (W2,D2,T2).` ensures no two sightings share the same witness, date, and town.
4. **Clue 1**: The constraint `:- sighting("Benny Baron", D1, _), sighting("Dan Delgado", D2, _), D1 != D2 + 2.` ensures Benny Baron's sighting date is exactly 2 days after Dan Delgado's.
5. **Clue 2**: The constraint forbids assignments where neither the August 7 sighting is by Gil Gates nor the August 5 sighting is from Walnut Creek, and vice versa.
6. **Clue 3**: The constraint `:- sighting(_, D_cc, "Crescent City"), sighting(_, D_is, "Islesboro"), D_cc != D_is + 1.` ensures the Crescent City sighting is exactly one day after the Islesboro sighting.
7. **Clue 4**: The constraint `:- sighting(W, D, "Walnut Creek"), D != 7, W != "Edith Estes".` ensures the Walnut Creek sighting is either on August 7 (date 7) or reported by Edith Estes.

This ASP program encodes all puzzle constraints and will yield a unique solution when solved with Clingo, matching each witness to their sighting date and town. The `#show sighting/3.` directive outputs the solution as a list of (witness, date, town) tuples.

```asp
% Constants
witness("Benny Baron"; "Dan Delgado"; "Edith Estes"; "Gil Gates").
date(4; 5; 6; 7).
town("Crescent City"; "Embden"; "Islesboro"; "Walnut Creek").

% Search space: for each witness, assign exactly one date and one town.
{ sighting(W, D, T) : date(D), town(T) } = 1 :- witness(W).

% Uniqueness: no two sightings share the same witness, date, or town.
{ W1=W2; D1=D2; T1=T2 } = 0 :- sighting(W1,D1,T1), sighting(W2,D2,T2), (W1,D1,T1) != (W2,D2,T2).

% 1. Benny Baron's sighting was submitted 2 days after Dan Delgado's report.
:- sighting("Benny Baron", D1, _), sighting("Dan Delgado", D2, _), D1 != D2 + 2.

% 2. Of the August 7 report and the August 5 sighting, one was reported by Gil Gates and the other was from Walnut Creek.
% If Gil Gates reported on August 7, then August 5 must be from Walnut Creek.
:- sighting("Gil Gates", 7, _), sighting(_, 5, T), T != "Walnut Creek".
% If August 7 sighting is from Walnut Creek, then August 5 must be reported by Gil Gates.
:- sighting(_, 7, "Walnut Creek"), sighting(W, 5, _), W != "Gil Gates".

% 3. The report from Crescent City was submitted 1 day after the sighting from Islesboro.
:- sighting(_, D_cc, "Crescent City"), sighting(_, D_is, "Islesboro"), D_cc != D_is + 1.

% 4. The sighting from Walnut Creek was either the August 7 report or Edith Estes's sighting.
:- sighting(W, D, "Walnut Creek"), D != 7, W != "Edith Estes".

#show sighting/3.
```

```asp
% Constants
witness("Benny Baron"; "Dan Delgado"; "Edith Estes"; "Gil Gates").
date(4; 5; 6; 7).
town("Crescent City"; "Embden"; "Islesboro"; "Walnut Creek").

% Search space: for each witness, assign exactly one date and one town.
{ sighting(W, D, T) : date(D), town(T) } = 1 :- witness(W).

% Uniqueness: no two sightings share the same witness, date, or town.
{ W1=W2; D1=D2; T1=T2 } = 0 :- sighting(W1,D1,T1), sighting(W2,D2,T2), (W1,D1,T1) != (W2,D2,T2).

% 1. Benny Baron's sighting was submitted 2 days after Dan Delgado's report.
:- sighting("Benny Baron", D1, _), sighting("Dan Delgado", D2, _), D1 != D2 + 2.

% 2. Of the August 7 report and the August 5 sighting, one was reported by Gil Gates and the other was from Walnut Creek.
% If Gil Gates reported on August 7, then August 5 must be from Walnut Creek.
:- sighting("Gil Gates", 7, _), sighting(_, 5, T), T != "Walnut Creek".
% If August 7 sighting is from Walnut Creek, then August 5 must be reported by Gil Gates.
:- sighting(_, 7, "Walnut Creek"), sighting(W, 5, _), W != "Gil Gates".
% Enforce that at least one of the two cases holds (either (Gil Gates on 7 and Aug 5 Walnut) or (Aug 7 Walnut and Gil Gates on 5))
:- not (sighting("Gil Gates", 7, _)), not (sighting(_, 5, "Walnut Creek")), not (sighting(_, 7, "Walnut Creek")), not (sighting("Gil Gates", 5, _)).

% 3. The report from Crescent City was submitted 1 day after the sighting from Islesboro.
:- sighting(_, D_cc, "Crescent City"), sighting(_, D_is, "Islesboro"), D_cc != D_is + 1.

% 4. The sighting from Walnut Creek was either the August 7 report or Edith Estes's sighting.
:- sighting(W, D, "Walnut Creek"), D != 7, W != "Edith Estes".

#show sighting/3.
```

refinements.response:
```asp
% Constants
witness("Benny Baron"; "Dan Delgado"; "Edith Estes"; "Gil Gates").
date(4; 5; 6; 7).
town("Crescent City"; "Embden"; "Islesboro"; "Walnut Creek").

% Search space: for each witness, assign exactly one date and one town.
{ sighting(W, D, T) : date(D), town(T) } = 1 :- witness(W).

% Uniqueness: no two sightings share the same witness, date, or town.
{ W1=W2; D1=D2; T1=T2 } = 0 :- sighting(W1,D1,T1), sighting(W2,D2,T2), (W1,D1,T1) != (W2,D2,T2).

% 1. Benny Baron's sighting was submitted 2 days after Dan Delgado's report.
:- sighting("Benny Baron", D1, _), sighting("Dan Delgado", D2, _), D1 != D2 + 2.

% 2. Of the August 7 report and the August 5 sighting, one was reported by Gil Gates and the other was from Walnut Creek.
% If Gil Gates reported on August 7, then August 5 must be from Walnut Creek.
:- sighting("Gil Gates", 7, _), sighting(_, 5, T), T != "Walnut Creek".
% If August 7 sighting is from Walnut Creek, then August 5 must be reported by Gil Gates.
:- sighting(_, 7, "Walnut Creek"), sighting(W, 5, _), W != "Gil Gates".
% Enforce that at least one of the two cases holds (either (Gil Gates on 7 and Aug 5 Walnut) or (Aug 7 Walnut and Gil Gates on 5))
:- not sighting("Gil Gates", 7, _), not sighting(_, 5, "Walnut Creek"), not sighting(_, 7, "Walnut Creek"), not sighting("Gil Gates", 5, _).

% 3. The report from Crescent City was submitted 1 day after the sighting from Islesboro.
:- sighting(_, D_cc, "Crescent City"), sighting(_, D_is, "Islesboro"), D_cc != D_is + 1.

% 4. The sighting from Walnut Creek was either the August 7 report or Edith Estes's sighting.
:- sighting(W, D, "Walnut Creek"), D != 7, W != "Edith Estes".

#show sighting/3.
```

date(4; 5; 6; 7).
town("Crescent City"; "Embden"; "Islesboro"; "Walnut Creek").

% Search space: for each witness, assign exactly one date and one town.
{ sighting(W, D, T) : date(D), town(T) } = 1 :- witness(W).

% Uniqueness: no two sightings share the same witness, date, or town.
{ W1=W2; D1=D2; T1=T2 } = 0 :- sighting(W1,D1,T1), sighting(W2,D2,T2), (W1,D1,T1) != (W2,D2,T2).

:- sighting("Benny Baron", D1, _), sighting("Dan Delgado", D2, _), D1 != D2 + 2.

% If Gil Gates reported on August 7, then August 5 must be from Walnut Creek.
:- sighting("Gil Gates", 7, _), sighting(_, 5, T), T != "Walnut Creek".
% If August 7 sighting is from Walnut Creek, then August 5 must be reported by Gil Gates.
:- sighting(_, 7, "Walnut Creek"), sighting(W, 5, _), W != "Gil Gates".

```asp
% Constants
witness("Benny Baron"; "Dan Delgado"; "Edith Estes"; "Gil Gates").
date(4; 5; 6; 7).
town("Crescent City"; "Embden"; "Islesboro"; "Walnut Creek").

% Search space: for each witness, assign exactly one date and one town.
{ sighting(W, D, T) : date(D), town(T) } = 1 :- witness(W).

% Uniqueness: no two sightings share the same witness, date, or town.
{ W1=W2; D1=D2; T1=T2 } = 0 :- sighting(W1,D1,T1), sighting(W2,D2,T2), (W1,D1,T1) != (W2,D2,T2).

% 1. Benny Baron's sighting was submitted 2 days after Dan Delgado's report.
:- sighting("Benny Baron", D1, _), sighting("Dan Delgado", D2, _), D1 != D2 + 2.

% 2. Of the August 7 report and the August 5 sighting, one was reported by Gil Gates and the other was from Walnut Creek.
% If Gil Gates reported on August 7, then August 5 must be from Walnut Creek.
:- sighting("Gil Gates", 7, _), sighting(_, 5, T), T != "Walnut Creek".
% If August 7 sighting is from Walnut Creek, then August 5 must be reported by Gil Gates.
:- sighting(_, 7, "Walnut Creek"), sighting(W, 5, _), W != "Gil Gates".

% 3. The report from Crescent City was submitted 1 day after the sighting from Islesboro.
:- sighting(_, D_cc, "Crescent City"), sighting(_, D_is, "Islesboro"), D_cc != D_is + 1.

% 4. The sighting from Walnut Creek was either the August 7 report or Edith Estes's sighting.
:- sighting(W, D, "Walnut Creek"), D != 7, W != "Edith Estes".

% Ensure exactly one of the two cases for constraint 2 holds (replacing incorrect line 21)
% This enforces that either (Gil on 7 and Aug5 Walnut) OR (Aug7 Walnut and Gil on 5)
:- not (sighting("Gil Gates", 7, _) and sighting(_, 5, "Walnut Creek")), not (sighting(_, 7, "Walnut Creek") and sighting("Gil Gates", 5, _)).

#show sighting/3.
```

refinements.response:
```asp
% Constants
witness("Benny Baron"; "Dan Delgado"; "Edith Estes"; "Gil Gates").
date(4; 5; 6; 7).
town("Crescent City"; "Embden"; "Islesboro"; "Walnut Creek").

% Search space: for each witness, assign exactly one date and one town.
{ sighting(W, D, T) : date(D), town(T) } = 1 :- witness(W).

% Uniqueness: no two sightings share the same witness, date, or town.
{ W1=W2; D1=D2; T1=T2 } = 0 :- sighting(W1,D1,T1), sighting(W2,D2,T2), (W1,D1,T1) != (W2,D2,T2).

% 1. Benny Baron's sighting was submitted 2 days after Dan Delgado's report.
:- sighting("Benny Baron", D1, _), sighting("Dan Delgado", D2, _), D1 != D2 + 2.

% 2. Of the August 7 report and the August 5 sighting, one was reported by Gil Gates and the other was from Walnut Creek.
% If Gil Gates reported on August 7, then August 5 must be from Walnut Creek.
:- sighting("Gil Gates", 7, _), sighting(_, 5, T), T != "Walnut Creek".
% If August 7 sighting is from Walnut Creek, then August 5 must be reported by Gil Gates.
:- sighting(_, 7, "Walnut Creek"), sighting(W, 5, _), W != "Gil Gates".

% 3. The report from Crescent City was submitted 1 day after the sighting from Islesboro.
:- sighting(_, D_cc, "Crescent City"), sighting(_, D_is, "Islesboro"), D_cc != D_is + 1.

% 4. The sighting from Walnut Creek was either the August 7 report or Edith Estes's sighting.
:- sighting(W, D, "Walnut Creek"), D != 7, W != "Edith Estes".

% Ensure exactly one of the two cases for constraint 2 holds (replacing incorrect line 21)
% This enforces that either (Gil on 7 and Aug5 Walnut) OR (Aug7 Walnut and Gil on 5)
:- not (sighting("Gil Gates", 7, _), sighting(_, 5, "Walnut Creek")), not (sighting(_, 7, "Walnut Creek"), sighting("Gil Gates", 5, _)).

#show sighting/3.
```
